Name: adaptive_kv_cache_optimization
Title: Adaptive KV Cache Optimization for Efficient Large Language Model Inference
Short Hypothesis: Adaptive optimization of key-value (KV) cache configurations can significantly reduce latency and improve throughput in large language model (LLM) inference without compromising accuracy, leveraging real-time performance profiling.
Related Work: Existing research like KVTuner discusses layer-wise KV cache quantization for LLM inference, but primarily focuses on pre-calibrated configurations rather than dynamic, real-time adaptive optimizations based on workload characteristics. This proposal distinguishes itself by proposing a real-time adaptive mechanism that adjusts KV cache configurations based on current inference workload and GPU performance metrics.
Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in latency-sensitive applications, where efficient inference is crucial. This proposal introduces an adaptive key-value (KV) cache optimization strategy that tailors cache configurations in real-time based on workload characteristics and GPU performance metrics. By profiling latency, throughput, and memory bandwidth during inference, we aim to dynamically adjust the precision and organization of KV caches. This adaptive approach is designed to mitigate memory bandwidth bottlenecks and improve overall inference speed without sacrificing model accuracy. The proposed method will be evaluated against state-of-the-art static KV cache optimization techniques, with performance metrics focused on p50/p99 latency, throughput, and energy efficiency. The outcome of this research could yield a significant advancement in LLM inference efficiency, establishing a novel standard for adaptive resource management in deep learning systems.
Experiments: 1. Implement a baseline KV cache configuration using existing static optimization techniques.
2. Develop the adaptive KV cache optimization framework that profiles GPU performance metrics (occupancy, bandwidth usage, etc.) and adjusts cache configurations in real-time.
3. Conduct A/B testing to compare the baseline and adaptive configurations under various inference workloads, measuring p50/p99 latency, throughput (tokens-per-second), and energy consumption.
4. Analyze the performance impact of different adaptive strategies (e.g., varying precision levels, cache size adjustments) on inference metrics to identify optimal configurations.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Potential risks include the complexity of real-time profiling and adjustment mechanisms, which may introduce overhead that could negate the benefits of adaptive optimization. Additionally, the success of the approach may vary across different models and hardware setups, necessitating extensive validation.
